calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar May-02-2018 Vacated

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Mar-01-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ross McLauren Madden, Esq. 415-567-1212 for Plaintiff Nicholas Hovland.
Landa Low, Deputy Attorney 510-433-9100 of the legal division of The CA Dept. of Transportation for Defendant State of California.
David Delbon, Deputy City Attorney 415-554-3800 from the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues GC 830.6 and case law. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is granted. CCSF presented sufficient evidence that the design of the public property where the accident occurred was approved by a CCSF engineer exercising discretion and the approval was reasonable. The declaration of the plaintiffs' expert contradicting the reasonableness of the approval is insufficient to defeat immunity. Plaintiffs failed to identify any change in physical conditions of the property after the design approval and before the incident. The trap exception of Government Code 830.8 does not defeat the design immunity of Government Code 830.6. Fairly read and as explained by later authorities, Cameron holds that the trap exception in section 830.8 is a separate ground for liability only when a plaintiff alleges failure to warn of a condition that was not part of any design or plan that is entitled to immunity under section 830.6 and was in fact a hidden, dangerous condition. Compton and Weinstein are not inconsistent with Cameron and are binding on this court. This case is similar to Weinstein. The Hovlands allege that the merge conditions where vehicles exiting from I-280 northbound flow into San Jose Avenue create a "trap" for anyone trying to turn into Rousseau Street and that CCSF failed to warn motorists of this dangerous condition. These merge conditions include the alleged unreasonable 45 mph speed, two off-ramps instead of one, lack of deceleration lane, lack of warning signs and inadequate distance to merge to the right. The design of these merge conditions at the location of the accident was reasonably approved and thus CCSF is entitled to immunity per section 830.6.

Order is signed (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Mar-01-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ross McLauren Madden, Esq. 415-567-1212 for Plaintiff Nicholas Hovland.
Landa Low, Deputy Attorney 510-433-9100 of the legal division of The CA Dept. of Transportation for Defendant State of California.
David Delbon, Deputy City Attorney 415-554-3800 from the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues GC 830.6 and case law. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Defendant State of California's motion for summary judgment is granted. The State presented sufficient evidence that the design of the public property where the accident occurred was approved by a State engineer exercising discretion and the approval was reasonable. The declaration of the plaintiffs' expert contradicting the reasonableness of the approval is insufficient to defeat immunity. Plaintiffs failed to identify any change in physical conditions of the property after the design approval and before the incident. The trap exception of Government Code 830.8 does not defeat the design immunity of Government Code 830.6. Fairly read and as explained by later authorities, Cameron holds that the trap exception in section 830.8 is a separate ground for liability only when a plaintiff alleges failure to warn of a condition that was not part of any design or plan that is entitled to immunity under section 830.6 and was in fact a hidden, dangerous condition. Compton and Weinstein are not inconsistent with Cameron and are binding on this court. This case is similar to Weinstein. The Hovlands allege that the merge conditions where vehicles exiting from I-280 northbound flow into San Jose Avenue create a "trap" for anyone trying to turn into Rousseau Street and that the State failed to warn motorists of this dangerous condition. These merge conditions include the alleged unreasonable 45 mph speed, two off-ramps instead of one, lack of deceleration lane, lack of warning signs and inadequate distance to merge to the right. The design of these merge conditions at the location of the accident was reasonably approved and thus the State is entitled to immunity per section 830.6.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/HK)
